chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: ISEN 302
Course Title: Economic Analysis of Engineering Projects
Section: 501 CRN 50294 / 502 CRN 11194/503 CRN11195/700 CRN 48287
Time: Sections: (501 - MW 10:20 am- 11:10 am), (502 – MW 12:40 pm – 1:30 pm), (503 MW 1:50 pm -2:40 pm)
Location: (All in person sections – Zach 344) and section 599 – ONLINE
Credit Hours: Credits 2-2 Lecture Hours

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor: Annette M. Rodgers
Office: ETB 4040
Phone: 979-845-2358
E-Mail: amrodgers@tamu.edu (Include ISEN 302 and your section number in your email subject line)
Office Hours: M & W 3:00 pm - 4:00 pm/ Tue 1:30 pm – 3:00 pm or by appointment in person or on Zoom
https://tamu.zoom.us/my/amrodgers

Outside of my office hours, you MUST set up an appointment. If you would like to set up a meeting, please email me 24 hours before the meeting time and provide a few times that might work with your schedule. I will try to find a time that works for both. You should prepare questions before we meet.

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: Principles of economic equivalence; time value of money; analysis of single and multiple investments; comparison of alternatives; capital recovery and after-tax analysis of economic projects

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: MATH 152 or MATH 172 and ability to use Microsoft Excel.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcome
chunks.content:
• Make engineering decisions based on an economic analysis of alternatives
• Explain and demonstrate the principles of economic equivalence and time value of money
• Formulate and solve equations using interest factors and formulas
• Distinguish between nominal and effective interest rates and convert between them
• Identify and determine constituent cash flows of single and/or multiple investments
• Examine, diagram, and compare investment alternatives on an economic basis using appropriate terminology
• Conclude and justify the best investment alternative(s) based on appropriate analysis
• Develop and construct spreadsheet models to investigate alternatives
• Determine and utilize appropriate capital recovery methods for alternatives

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Engineering Economic Analysis (14 th Edition)
D. Newnan, T. G. Eschenbach, J. P. Lavelle, and N. Lewis (February 19, 2019)
ISBN: 978-0-19-093191-9
Oxford University Press

We have been notified that this textbook is no longer available in print version. However, the edition is available with a new (ISBN: 9780190063467) as it is now included as a pack and contains an access code. The option I recommend is to rent the digital version of the 14th edition, at the bookstore or anywhere you can find it at a lesser price. I am not aware of what the bookstore charges for the book, but I am adding a link below where you may rent it for $49.99 if that price is better:

https://www.vitalsource.com/products/engineering-economic-analysis-don-newnan-ted-eschenbach-v9780190931940?term=9780190063467

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Homework assignments are due on the due date posted on Canvas. Please submit all work through Canvas. You should submit your work on time. Late submissions are penalized 10% per each late day and are only accepted on the week the work is due, after that, no late work will be accepted. Please note that homework assignments have different value and weight, so please do not assume they are all equal. In addition, there might be in-class assignments / checking for understanding, short quizzes, in-class quizzes, three tests during the semester, and one special assignment worth a test grade. No make-up examinations will be allowed unless prior arrangements have been made and only if you have a university accepted excuse. Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered “late” and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7), but this HAS to be pre-arranged with the professor.

The components of the course grade and their weights are as follows: Assignments, 20%; Quizzes, 20%; Tests, 60%. The university letter grade policy is followed (>=90%: A; 80-89%: B; 70 – 79%: C; 60 – 69%: D; below 60%: F.)

There will be no curve at the end of this semester, so please make sure you submit your work on time!

chunks.title: Other Course Information Items
chunks.content:
The course website will be maintained through http://canvas.tamu.edu/. This site will contain announcements and other information concerning the course. In addition, it will be used to distribute class materials (homework assignment, notes,) and grades. Check the website regularly, I will send all information and communications through Canvas through the “Announcements” section. Any information posted there will be as valid as if it was mentioned in class.

It is expected that all work submitted in this class will be done individually. If you got any help with homework, please cite that person in your write-up. For example, “I thank John Smith for helping me solve this problem.” If you worked together with one or more other people, then each of you should cite the other(s).

chunks.title: FE Exam / Calculator Policy
chunks.content: A major reason for taking this class is to prepare all engineering majors for the Fundamentals of Engineering (FE) exam, as well as to prepare Industrial Engineering majors for other ISEN courses. To this end, students are strongly encouraged to use calculators that are approved by NCEES for use on the FE. The NCEES website (http://ncees.org/exams/calculator-policy/) provides a list of the current approved calculator brands and models.

chunks.title: Exams / Proctoring
chunks.content: Most exams are administered through Canvas using Respondus proctoring software. They will be closed-book/notes/smartphone. Calculator is allowed if needed. If you have requested special accommodations, you might not need to schedule your test through the testing center (More information will be provided after the semester begins. Please come see me if you have questions.

chunks.title: Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility. Students are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused absences, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

chunks.title: Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of more than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed upon by the student and instructor" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.1).

"The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up work missed because of an unexcused absence" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2).

Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and Student Conduct Code. (See Student Rule 24.)

chunks.title: Academic Integrity Statement and Policy
chunks.content:
"An Aggie does not lie, cheat or steal, or tolerate those who do."

"Texas A&M University students are responsible for authenticating all work submitted to an instructor. If asked, students must be able to produce proof that the item submitted is indeed the work of that student. Students must keep appropriate records at all times. The inability to authenticate one's work, should the instructor request it, may be sufficient grounds to initiate an academic misconduct case" (Section 20.1.2.3, Student Rule 20).

You can learn more about the Aggie Honor System Office Rules and Procedures, academic integrity, and your rights and responsibilities at aggiehonor.tamu.edu.

chunks.title: COVID-19 Statement
chunks.content: To help protect Aggieland and stop the spread of COVID-19, Texas A&M University urges students to be vaccinated and to wear masks in classrooms and all other academic facilities on campus, including labs. Doing so exemplifies the Aggie Core Values of respect, leadership, integrity, and selfless service by putting community concerns above individual preferences. COVID-19 vaccines and masking regardless of vaccination status - have been shown to be safe and effective at reducing spread to others, infection, hospitalization, and death.
